_meta.hardware: Snapdragon X Elite CRD (Adreno X1-85 / Hexagon HTP)
_meta.epistemics_warning: ⚠️ All findings are hypotheses derived from limited models on 1 device (Snapdragon X Elite). Confidence levels reflect how well the mechanism is understood, not how universally applicable the finding is. ALWAYS re-validate on new model architectures before using to prune search space.

findings.title: opset 21 export gives +24-31% speedup on DINOv2-family models on QNN NPU — mechanism UNKNOWN, NOT a general ViT property, MobileViT benefit NOT reproduced on clean rerun
findings.observation: Catalog sweep 2026-06-13 + validation sweep 2026-06-16 (ORT 1.24.5, W8A16 quantized.onnx, 3×500-iter sessions): DINOv2-small +30.6% (opset17 7.18ms → opset21 4.98ms). DINOv2-base +24.1% (opset17 34.56ms → opset21 26.23ms). CRITICAL CONTROL: dino-vitb16 (plain DINO ViT-B/16) -0.7% — NEUTRAL. rad-dino (ViT-L medical) -0.1% — CPU-bound, no NPU effect. ViT-base: -7.4%. BERT/RoBERTa/DistilBERT: neutral. MobileViT-small: REVISED — the original +26.5% (2026-06-13) was on an inflated ~12ms baseline. Clean from-scratch 11-hypothesis rerun 2026-06-22 (fresh winml config+build, 3×500-iter) gave baseline (h0 opset17) median 5.51ms and h3 opset21 median 5.355ms = +2.81% with FULLY OVERLAPPING session ranges (h0=[4.98,5.51,5.72] vs h3=[5.36,5.26,5.90]) → npu-001 NEUTRAL on MobileViT. h6 (opset21+matmul_transpose), previously cited as +42.1%, was 6.218ms = SLOWER than baseline. The earlier MobileViT speedup was a thermal/DVFS artifact of a slow baseline, not an opset21 effect.
findings.mechanism_invalidation: Original hypothesis: kMaxSupportedOpset < 21 gate causes NHWC bypass on older ORT. INVALIDATED: sweep used onnxruntime-windowsml==1.24.5 where kMaxSupportedOpset >= 22. Both opset 17 and opset 21 go through the same NHWC layout transform path on this ORT version. The bypass mechanism does NOT apply. The observed speedup is real but the cause is unknown.
findings.mechanism_source: ORT source code investigation (2026-06-10) for ORT < 1.18. Sweep used onnxruntime-windowsml==1.24.5 where this mechanism no longer applies.
findings.ort_version_critical_note: The original mechanism (kMaxSupportedOpset gate in IsSupportedOpset()) requires kMaxSupportedOpset < 21. onnxruntime-windowsml==1.24.5 (ORT 1.24.x) has kMaxSupportedOpset >= 22, so BOTH opset17 and opset21 go through the NHWC layout transform. The bypass mechanism does NOT apply to the ORT version used in the sweep. The observed speedup for DINOv2 and MobileViT has an UNKNOWN root cause.
architecture_requirement: empirically: DINOv2 family (facebook/dinov2-*) consistently benefits. Plain ViT (dino-vitb16) does NOT. Hybrid Conv+attention (MobileViT) showed an apparent speedup in original data but did NOT reproduce on clean rerun (neutral). Pure Conv (ResNet) insufficient data. NLP: neutral.
critical_caveats: MECHANISM UNKNOWN: Transpose count is IDENTICAL in opset17 and opset21 (both 49 nodes on dinov2-small). The original Transpose-elimination hypothesis is RULED OUT. The +48 Reshape nodes in opset21 are the most observable structural difference but why this speeds up QNN NPU is not understood.
RESNET-18 EXCLUDED: apparent +20% is statistical noise — 3 sessions span 4x range at sub-ms latency. Need 3 sessions × 2000 iters for reliable data at this scale.
DVFS NOISE: always use 3 sessions × 500+ iters with cool-down. Single-session CV is meaningless on QNN NPU.
SCOPE IS DINOV2-FAMILY NOT GENERAL VIT: dino-vitb16 (same ViT-B size as dinov2-base) shows -0.7% NEUTRAL. The speedup is DINOv2-architecture-specific.

benefits_from_opset21: facebook/dinov2-base (+24.1%, validation sweep 2026-06-16, fresh quantized.onnx builds, 3-session h1=[34.56,34.67,33.15]ms h3=[33.00,26.22,26.23]ms)
no_benefit_neutral: apple/mobilevit-small: REVISED to NEUTRAL. Original +42.1% (h6) / +26.5% (h3) was measured against an inflated ~12ms baseline. Clean from-scratch rerun 2026-06-22 (3×500-iter): baseline h0 opset17 5.51ms, h3 opset21 5.355ms = +2.81% with overlapping session ranges; h6 (opset21+matmul_transpose) 6.218ms = SLOWER. The earlier 'win' was a DVFS/thermal baseline artifact.
facebook/dino-vitb16 (-0.7%, validation sweep 2026-06-16, h1=[19.92,19.97,19.90]ms h3=[20.20,20.07,19.99]ms — NEUTRAL, critical control)
rizvandwiki/gender-classification (+3.5% apparent, ranges overlap 13.89/13.92ms, NEUTRAL — plain ViT, CRITICAL: near-identical op counts to DINOv2-small (49 Transpose, 121 Reshape) yet NO benefit)
marginal_inconclusive: BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5 (+7.3%, h0=[10.52,10.32,11.01]ms h3=[10.25,9.33,9.94]ms — ranges barely non-overlapping but CV=0.3; NOT CONFIRMED. Needs 5+ sessions to differentiate from noise. Unusual for BERT architecture; all other NLP models tested at <1%)
not_benchmarked_predicted_neutral: openai/clip-vit-base-patch32 — build failed at quantization (feature-extraction task calibration not supported); pure transformer, expected neutral based on all NLP data
cardiffnlp/twitter-roberta-base-sentiment-latest — not run; RoBERTa architecture, predicted neutral (consistent with roberta-base-squad2 +0.1%)
distilbert/distilbert-base-cased-distilled-squad — not run; DistilBERT architecture, predicted neutral (consistent with distilbert-base-uncased -0.1%)
cpu_bound_cannot_test: microsoft/rad-dino (-0.1% on CPU EP, all hypotheses ~275ms CV<0.022 — model runs on CPU, opset irrelevant; QNN NPU BUILD_FAIL 2026-06-17, see npu-008)
data_unreliable: resnet-18 — sub-ms latency, 3-session range spans 4x; no reliable signal (see data_reliability_notes)

original_mechanism_explanation.root_cause_for_old_ort: kMaxSupportedOpset gate in IsSupportedOpset() (onnxruntime/core/optimizer/layout_transformation/layout_transformation.cc). On ORT where kMaxSupportedOpset < 21, opset 21 models bypass the NCHW→NHWC layout transformer entirely.
original_mechanism_explanation.why_bypass_helped_convnext: NHWC layout transform inserts Transpose(NCHW→NHWC) around Conv. For ConvNext, residual connections prevent Transpose cancellation → opset17 graph has MORE Transposes on HTP than opset21 graph.
original_mechanism_explanation.why_cpu_is_opposite: CPU relies on TransposeOptimizer to REMOVE existing Transposes. Skipping the optimizer (opset > kMaxSupportedOpset) leaves Transposes in place → CPU SLOWER. Same gate, opposite effect.

ort_kMaxSupportedOpset_by_version.v1.24.x: >= 23 (CONFIRMED: ORT 1.24.4 in C:\tmp\autoconfig-demo accepts opset 22 and 23 via InferenceSession with CPUExecutionProvider; opset 24 fails with 'No op registered for ...' not 'Unsupported opset')

transpose_analysis_2026_06_16.method: onnx.load() on winml-built optimized.onnx and quantized.onnx for h0 (opset17) and h3 (opset21) from catalog_qnn_sweep facebook--dinov2-small. Op counts via collections.Counter on graph.node.
transpose_analysis_2026_06_16.key_finding: Transpose count is IDENTICAL (49 nodes) in both opset17 and opset21. The NHWC Transpose-reduction hypothesis is RULED OUT. opset21 has MORE Reshape nodes (+48), more QDQ pairs (+48 DQ, +48 Q), and more total nodes. Despite more nodes, opset21 runs 30% faster on QNN NPU — mechanism still unknown.
rules_out: NHWC Transpose elimination as speedup cause
consistent_with: Different graph structure at opset21 enabling better QNN NPU internal scheduling or graph partitioning, possibly via the +48 Reshape nodes acting as data-layout hints or memory access pattern changes

alternative_mechanism_hypotheses: QNN EP graph partitioner assigns ops differently when the model has opset21 Reshape semantics — the +48 Reshape nodes may segment the graph into better-aligned HTP subgraphs
Quantization calibration path differs between opset exports → quantized.onnx has different scale/zero-point distributions at opset21 → better QNN NPU numeric alignment
PyTorch ONNX exporter produces different intermediate tensor shapes at opset 21 → better memory access locality on QNN NPU HBM
The +48 Reshape ops in opset21 are 'free' no-ops on QNN NPU (identity reshape with same shape) that happen to trigger a faster QNN internal code path

dinov2_small.assessment: RELIABLE. Ranges barely overlap only at extremes. h3 sessions 1+2 (4.97/4.88ms) are well below entire h1 range. Speedup is real.

dinov2_base_v3.assessment: RELIABLE. h1 sessions fully consistent (~34ms). h3 s0 slightly elevated (JIT warmup) but s1+s2 consistent at 26.2ms. Speedup +24.1% is well-separated from noise.
dinov2_base_v3.tool: validation_sweep.py v3, quantized.onnx W8A16 (fresh builds for both hyps)

dino_vitb16.assessment: RELIABLE CONTROL. Extremely stable. +0.7% regression (within noise). Opset21 has NO EFFECT on plain DINO ViT-B/16. Critical discriminant: npu-001 speedup is NOT a general ViT property.

mobilevit_small.assessment: REVISED to UNRELIABLE/NEUTRAL. The original h1 (opset17) median ~11.7ms was inflated by a 27.4ms DVFS spike, making opset21 look ~20-26% faster. A clean from-scratch 11-hypothesis rerun 2026-06-22 (fresh winml config+build, 3×500-iter) measured a true baseline median of 5.51ms; h3 opset21 = 5.355ms = +2.81% with FULLY OVERLAPPING session ranges → effect-size gate verdict NEUTRAL_WITHIN_NOISE. h6 (opset21+matmul_transpose, previously cited +42.1%) = 6.218ms = SLOWER. The original 'speedup' was a polluted-baseline / DVFS artifact, not an opset21 effect.

resnet_18.assessment: UNRELIABLE. Sub-ms model. Session range spans 4x for same config. Reported '+20.2% speedup' (h1 median 2.72ms vs h3 median 2.18ms) is NOT a real signal — the two distributions fully overlap. REMOVED from benefits list.

gender_classification_vit.assessment: NEUTRAL. Ranges barely not overlapping (h0 min=13.89ms, h3 max=13.92ms). +3.5% is within DVFS noise (CV ~0.35). CRITICAL: this ViT model has IDENTICAL op counts to DINOv2-small (49 Transpose, 121 Reshape, ~72 Gemm) yet shows NO benefit. Confirms npu-001 is not explainable by op-count or general ViT architecture.

bge_small_en.assessment: MARGINAL / INCONCLUSIVE. Ranges barely not overlapping but CV ~0.3 means high within-session variance. +7.3% apparent gain — larger than all other NLP models (distilbert -0.1%, MiniLM -0.7%, RoBERTa +0.1%) but may be DVFS noise. Needs 5+ sessions to confirm. Do NOT cite as benefit.

findings.action_for_autoconfig: Include opset 21 in search for DINOv2-family models (facebook/dinov2-*). Do NOT assume it helps MobileViT-class Conv+attention hybrids — the original MobileViT win did NOT reproduce on a clean rerun (neutral, +2.81% within noise). Do NOT apply to plain ViT (dino-vitb16, gender-classification both neutral), YOLOS, or NLP (BERT-family all neutral at ±0.7%). CRITICAL: gender-classification ViT has IDENTICAL op counts to DINOv2-small (49 Transpose, 121 Reshape) but shows NO benefit — the effect is deeper than op counts. For ResNet-class Conv-only: insufficient data. ALWAYS dump optimized graph to compare Transpose counts if speedup is unexpected, and ALWAYS clear the effect-size gate (gain >= 2×session-CV AND ranges separated) before trusting a win.
findings.confidence: medium-high on empirical observation (DINOv2-small +30.6% and DINOv2-base +24.1% both confirmed with clean 3-session protocol, fresh builds). Low on mechanism — original Transpose-bypass explanation ruled out (Transpose count identical opset17/21), kMaxSupportedOpset>=23 confirmed. Mechanism unknown. Scope: DINOv2 family only until mechanism is understood. 12 models now tested: 2 benefit (DINOv2-small/base), 8 neutral (incl. MobileViT after clean 2026-06-22 rerun), 1 marginal/inconclusive (BGE-small +7.3% with high CV), 1 CPU-bound.
findings.scope: ORT 1.24.5 (onnxruntime-windowsml). DINOv2-small and DINOv2-base confirmed. MobileViT-small REVISED to NEUTRAL (original win was a DVFS baseline artifact; clean rerun 2026-06-22 = +2.81% within noise). Does NOT apply to plain ViT (dino-vitb16 and rizvandwiki/gender-classification both confirmed NEUTRAL despite identical op counts to DINOv2-small), YOLOS-small, BERT-family NLP, CPU-bound models (rad-dino). ResNet-18 data inconclusive. BGE-small-en +7.3% marginal, inconclusive.

perf_gain_validation_gates.gate1_statistical: PASSED for DINOv2 (3-session, ranges separate). FAILED for MobileViT (clean rerun 2026-06-22: ranges overlap, +2.81% < effect-size noise floor → NEUTRAL). FAILED for ResNet-18.
perf_gain_validation_gates.gate2_mechanism: FAILED — original kMaxSupportedOpset bypass mechanism does not apply to ORT 1.24.x. New mechanism uninvestigated.
perf_gain_validation_gates.gate3_thermal_control: PARTIALLY — 3×500-iter with 30s cool-down is better than single-session but DVFS spikes still occur and CAN poison the baseline (the MobileViT win was traced to exactly this; see ep_knowledge/README.md promotion checklist).

follow_up_required: DONE: kMaxSupportedOpset >= 23 confirmed for ORT 1.24.4 (accepts opset 22 and 23 at InferenceSession level)
DONE: Transpose analysis — opset17 vs opset21 DINOv2-small: IDENTICAL (49 Transpose both). Not the mechanism.
OPEN: Investigate QNN EP graph partitioning diff for opset17 vs opset21. Why do +48 Reshape nodes help?
Run 5+ sessions (not 3) on DINOv2 opset17 vs opset21 to reduce DVFS uncertainty
Test EfficientNet-B0, MobileNet-V3 to determine if benefit is 'Conv+residual' or 'Conv+attention hybrid' specific
For ResNet-18: run 3 sessions x 2000 iters to get reliable sub-ms measurements

findings.title: W8A16 quantization provides ~1.9x speedup over FP32 on QNN NPU (ConvNext only — not yet generalized)
findings.observation: ConvNext FP32 baseline: p50=19.4ms. W8A16 quantized (minmax, 128 samples): p50=10.29ms. 1 model, 1 device.
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: QNN HTP has native INT8 weight / FP16 activation datapath. W8A16 maps directly to HTP's weight-compressed matmul kernels.
findings.action_for_autoconfig: Always quantize for QNN NPU. W8A16 is the starting point. Validate accuracy after quantization.
findings.confidence: medium — mechanism is well-understood (HTP architecture), but 1.9x magnitude is from 1 model only. Speedup will vary by architecture.
findings.scope: ConvNext only — single model validation. The catalog sweep used W8A16 for all 8 models but did not include FP32 baselines for those models, so the 1.9x figure cannot be generalized. Need FP32 baseline runs on at least 3 diverse models before claiming 'most vision models'.
findings.do_not_generalize_to: Models with unusual op types not supported by QNN W8A16 path. Magnitude claim (1.9x) is ConvNext-specific.
follow_up_required: Measure FP32 baseline for MobileViT, DINOv2, ResNet-18 to verify speedup generalizes

findings.title: winml compile adds ~1.7x speedup on top of quantization for QNN NPU (ConvNext only — not yet generalized)
findings.observation: ConvNext W8A16 quantized: p50=10.29ms. W8A16 + compiled (EPContext): p50=6.01ms. 1 model, 1 device.
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: Compilation pre-builds the QNN binary graph (.bin) and eliminates JIT graph partitioning at session creation time. EPContext model loads the pre-built binary directly.
findings.action_for_autoconfig: Always run winml compile after finding best quantized config for QNN NPU.
findings.confidence: medium — mechanism is well-understood (EPContext documented by QNN SDK). 1.7x magnitude is ConvNext-specific. Simpler models may see less benefit; complex models may see more.
findings.scope: ConvNext only — single model validation. Mechanism generalizes; magnitude (1.7x) does not. The catalog sweep results.json baseline p50 values already include the effects of whatever auto-config winml chose (which may or may not include compile) — not directly comparable.
follow_up_required: Verify compile speedup on MobileViT and DINOv2

findings.title: ⚠️ ANECDOTE (NO DATA): W8A8 may cause accuracy collapse on models with LN+GELU — UNVALIDATED
findings.observation: W8A8 quantization was attempted on ConvNext. The experiment was aborted early — exact accuracy numbers were NOT recorded. The claim 'top-1 < 15%' is a recalled anecdote from the experimenter, not a measured result.
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: ConvNext uses LayerNormalization + GELU in every block. Quantizing both weights AND activations to INT8 in these ops introduces severe numerical error. However, this is a hypothesis — the aborted experiment does not confirm or refute it.
findings.action_for_autoconfig: Treat as anecdotal. Do NOT use this to skip W8A8 without running eval first. If W8A8 top-1 drops > 15 points vs W8A16 baseline on first attempt, then skip.
findings.confidence: very_low — anecdotal, no preserved data, experiment not reproducible as recorded
findings.scope: UNVALIDATED. May apply to models with LN+GELU blocks but this is unconfirmed.
findings.do_not_generalize_to: BERT/ResNet models where W8A8 is often fine
findings.required_experiment: Run W8A8 quantization on ConvNext-tiny-224, record exact top-1 accuracy (eval on ImageNet-1k, 1000 samples minimum). Compare to W8A16 baseline. If collapse observed, also run with calibration_method=percentile to see if calibration quality is the issue.

findings.title: QNN Hub W8A16 model is slower on ORT QNN EP stack than ORT-quantized W8A16 — but comparison is not fair
findings.observation: QNN Hub W8A16 on winml ORT QNN EP: p50=14.82ms, std=8.8ms. ORT-quantized W8A16 (opset 17 QDQ): p50=6.01ms stable.
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: QNN Hub uses opset 21 QDQ format with uint16 input tensor — this format may be incompatible with ORT QNN EP's expected quantization format.
findings.fairness_caveat: ⚠️ This is NOT a fair comparison. QNN Hub models are compiled for the qairt native stack (qualcomm AI runtime), not for ORT QNN EP. Running a qairt-compiled model through ORT QNN EP is an unsupported use case. The comparison only shows that you should use ORT-generated quantization when targeting ORT QNN EP — which is obvious.
findings.action_for_autoconfig: Use ORT-generated W8A16 quantization (winml build), NOT QNN Hub pre-quantized models, when targeting ORT QNN EP stack.
findings.confidence: low — the finding is trivially true (use the right tool for the right stack) but the experiment doesn't tell us anything useful about relative performance.
findings.scope: ORT QNN EP stack only. QNN Hub models on their native qairt stack are likely much faster — that comparison was never made.

findings.title: Conv fusions (conv-bn/add/activation) cause catastrophic QNN NPU CPU fallback on Conv-dominant models
findings.observation: ResNet-18 with conv-bn-fusion+conv-add-fusion+conv-activation-fusion: 3-session p50s = [132.3, 134.97, 130.67]ms (CV=0.016, extremely stable) vs baseline [0.99, 4.00, 2.72]ms. ~130-135x regression. MobileViT with same fusions: [11.60, 11.36, 10.52]ms — neutral vs baseline [10.56, 11.72, 27.44]ms. BERT-family: neutral (no Conv ops to fuse). VALIDATION SWEEP 2026-06-16: dinov2-base h4=[26.06,25.92,25.87]ms vs h1=[34.56,34.67,33.15]ms → fusions actually -25% (FASTER, not regression). dino-vitb16 h4=[20.12,20.04,20.41]ms vs h1=[19.92,19.97,19.90]ms → +1.0% (neutral). Conv fusions are only hazardous for Conv-dominant models.
findings.session_evidence_note: The h4 sessions for ResNet-18 (132.3, 134.97, 130.67ms) show near-zero variance (CV=0.016) — in stark contrast to all other hypotheses. This is unusual for QNN NPU and strongly suggests deterministic CPU fallback (not DVFS noise). The regression is 50-136x even comparing best sessions.
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: ORT conv fusion pass (ConvAddActivationFusion, ConvBNFusion) produces fused op types (e.g., Conv+BN fused) that QNN EP cannot map to HTP kernels. These ops fall back to CPU execution, adding PCIe round-trip overhead per-op for a Conv-heavy graph like ResNet.
findings.action_for_autoconfig: ⚠️ CRITICAL: Do NOT apply conv-bn-fusion / conv-add-fusion / conv-activation-fusion for QNN NPU on Conv-dominant models (ResNet, EfficientNet, MobileNet). These passes are beneficial for CPU EP but hazardous for QNN NPU. Always run accuracy + latency gate after applying any Conv fusion. If regression > 5x, disable all conv fusions immediately.
findings.confidence: high on regression observation (4900%); medium on mechanism (CPU fallback hypothesis not yet confirmed via EP partition dump)
findings.scope: Any model containing Conv ops whose graph is changed by the full conv-fusion pack — NOT limited to Conv-dominant models. Originally scoped to Conv-dominant (ResNet/EfficientNet/MobileNet); the 2026-06-24 SegFormer result REFUTES the 'dominant-only' qualifier: SegFormer-b1 has only 1.5% Conv ops (conv stem / overlapping patch-embed) yet conv fusions still produced up to ~10x regression. MobileViT safe (original data). DINOv2 and plain ViT (no Conv stem): fusions neutral or slightly beneficial (2026-06-16 validation). Not applicable to pure-NLP transformers (no Conv ops to fuse — bench-skipped as no-ops).
findings.severity: critical — can produce up to ~10x (SegFormer) to ~135x (ResNet-18) regression
follow_up_required: Dump QNN EP partition to confirm fused ops cause CPU fallback
Test EfficientNet and MobileNet to confirm generalization
Check if winml analyze linter can detect this pattern pre-build
findings.refinement: 2026-06-17 delta sweep: ResNet-18 h10 (conv_add_fusion ONLY, no conv-bn or conv-activation) = p50 0.955ms vs baseline 0.964ms (+0.93%) — NEUTRAL. Catastrophic regression ONLY occurs with full fusion pack (conv-bn-fusion + conv-add-fusion + conv-activation-fusion) which produces FusedConv ops. Individual conv-add-fusion is safe. Root cause is confirmed: FusedConv op created by the bundle is not dispatchable by QNN EP.
findings.cross_arch_confirmation_2026_06_24: SegFormer-b1 (nvidia/segformer-b1-finetuned-ade-512-512, image-segmentation, W8A16) QNN NPU sweep independently reproduces npu-006 on a SECOND architecture family with a conv stem. baseline h0=20.19ms (screen). h4 (opset17 + conv fusions) = 83.75ms → gain -357.8% (~4.1x regression). h5 (opset21 + conv fusions) = 200.11ms → gain -899.3% (~10x regression). Both verdict=DISCARD. graph_changed=True for both (the fusion fired — these are real FusedConv graphs, not no-ops). results.json auto-flagged npu-006_regression=True. conv_pct=1.5%, proving the hazard is triggered by the PRESENCE of a fusible Conv subgraph, not Conv-dominance. By contrast, on pure-transformer models in the same sweep (koelectra, all-mpnet) the conv-fusion hypotheses were byte-identical no-ops (no Conv to fuse) and were bench-skipped.

findings.title: DVFS thermal noise on QNN NPU makes CV-based stability gating unreliable — requires session-level averaging
findings.observation: Across all 8 catalog models, QNN NPU CV ranges 0.1–2.0+ even on warm device. Original CV<15% gate blocks most candidates. Differences < 10% are within noise floor.
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: Snapdragon X Elite HTP Hexagon core runs DVFS aggressively. Single-session CV is dominated by thermal state, not model performance. The only reliable signal comes from session-level averaging (3+ independent sessions with cool-down).
findings.action_for_autoconfig: DISABLE CV gate for QNN NPU. Replace with: (1) minimum 3 independent sessions × 500+ iters with 30s cool-down between sessions. (2) Use median p50 across sessions as the signal. (3) Only trust gains > 10% — anything below is within noise floor. (4) Do NOT compare within-session std to declare stability.
findings.confidence: high — consistent across 8 models in catalog sweep
findings.scope: General — applies to all models on QNN NPU / Snapdragon X Elite HTP

bench_protocol_update.screen_phase: SKIP CV gate; run 200 iters as warmup only

findings.title: microsoft/rad-dino fails to build on QNN NPU across all opset variants (winml crash rc=0xC0000005)
findings.observation: catalog_qnn_sweep run 2026-06-17: all 6 hypotheses for microsoft/rad-dino (opset 17/19/21, with/without conv fusions) returned rc=3221225794 (0xC0000005, access violation) in <2s. No stderr captured — winml process crashed before producing any output. This is distinct from a build error: it is a hard crash of the winml CLI itself.
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: rad-dino is a ViT encoder with a non-standard DINOv2 variant (larger heads, custom CLS token handling). Likely contains one or more ONNX operators or graph shapes that trigger an unguarded null-dereference or out-of-bounds access in the QNN EP quantization or compilation path (winml build calls QNN SDK compilation under the hood). Could also be a model size / dynamic axis issue.
findings.action_for_autoconfig: Skip QNN NPU for microsoft/rad-dino. If QNN NPU is required, file a bug with the crash dump and test with winml analyze first to identify unsupported ops before attempting build.
findings.confidence: high on observation (reproducible across all 6 hypotheses in same run); low on mechanism (no stack trace available)
findings.scope: microsoft/rad-dino only (confirmed). DINOv2-family models in general (facebook/dinov2-small, facebook/dinov2-base) are NOT affected — they build and run on QNN NPU successfully.
findings.severity: blocker — model is incompatible with QNN NPU build
follow_up_required: Run winml analyze --ep qnn on rad-dino ONNX to check unsupported ops
Capture crash dump (ProcDump) to get stack trace
Compare ONNX graph structure of rad-dino vs facebook/dinov2-small to isolate differentiating ops

findings.title: bias_softmax_fusion adds incremental +14% on DINOv2 QNN NPU when combined with opset21
findings.confidence: medium
findings.scope: ViT-class models with attention+bias patterns. Confirmed on DINOv2-small; untested on plain ViT or BERT.
findings.observation: Catalog sweep 2026-06-17 delta sweep: DINOv2-small h7 (opset21+bias_softmax_fusion) p50=4.027ms vs h3 (opset21 only) p50=4.977ms. Incremental gain = +14.1% on top of opset21 alone. Total gain vs baseline: +38.6% (h7) vs +24.1% (h3). bias_softmax_fusion hypothesis also outperforms attention_fusion (h8=+28.4%) and matmul_transpose (h6=+24.8%) on DINOv2.
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: bias_softmax_fusion folds Add(qk_scores, bias)+Softmax into a single FusedSoftmax op. QNN Hexagon HTP has a native hardware path for fused attention-head softmax. Reduces dispatch overhead between the bias addition and the softmax kernel.
not_tested: BERT/RoBERTa (expected neutral based on npu-001 scope)
findings.autoconfig_action: For DINOv2-family: include bias_softmax_fusion in the opset21 bundle. Prioritize over attention_fusion (h8) since h7 outperformed h8 by 10 percentage points on DINOv2-small.
findings.source: catalog_qnn_sweep.py h6-h10 delta sweep, DINOv2-small results.json

findings.title: highdimRTR_lowdimRTR causes -19% regression on MobileViT QNN NPU due to spurious Reshape insertion
findings.confidence: high
findings.scope: Models with Gemm->Reshape->Transpose hybrid unfold patterns (MobileViT confirmed). DINOv2 (pure ViT) benefits: +38.1%. Architecture-dependent.
findings.observation: Catalog sweep 2026-06-17 delta sweep: MobileViT-small h9 (opset21+highdimRTR_lowdimRTR) median_p50=14.363ms vs h0 baseline 12.075ms = -18.9% regression. GPU sweep: same model h9 (GPU) = -6.89%. ONNX diff: h9 NPU graph has +36 extra Reshape nodes (395->431 total; 108->144 Reshape). The 12 original RTR patterns in h0 are UNCHANGED in h9. Instead, optimizer inserted Reshape pairs as intermediaries after Gemm nodes, breaking dispatch merging.
findings.mechanism_detail: highdimRTR misidentifies Gemm->Reshape->Transpose sequences (MobileViT CNN-ViT hybrid patch-unfold mechanism) as reducible RTR patterns. Inserts 36 extra intermediate Reshape nodes. These break Gemm+Reshape dispatch merging on QNN NPU and add DMA traffic. NPU more severely affected (-19%) than GPU (-6.9%) due to higher HTP DMA sensitivity.
validated_safe_models: facebook/dinov2-small (+38.1% NPU with h9 — pure ViT benefits from highdimRTR)
findings.architectural_discriminator: Gemm->Reshape->Transpose hybrid unfold pattern (CNN-ViT). Detect via analyze_insight.py op-sequence scan before applying.
findings.autoconfig_action: Hard-block highdimRTR for models with Gemm->Reshape->Transpose hybrid sequences. analyze_insight.py should detect this pattern and add highdimRTR to skip_set.
findings.source: catalog_qnn_sweep.py h6-h10 delta sweep + ONNX graph diff (MobileViT h0 vs h9 node count comparison)

findings.title: Fusions that fire (graph topology changes) but yield no perf benefit should be recorded and benefit-gated, not auto-kept
findings.confidence: medium
findings.scope: Cross-architecture on QNN NPU. Observed strongest on transformer/attention models (BERT, ConvNeXt) where conv/attention fusions fire cleanly but p50 is unchanged. Distinct from npu-006 (fusion fires AND regresses) and npu-001 (change helps).
findings.observation: A fusion flag can be confirmed *applicable* — after enabling it the post-optimize op count drops and graph topology changes vs the baseline build — yet the measured p50 delta stays inside the DVFS noise band (|delta| < CV-derived threshold). BERT-base h2/h3/h4 on NPU: graphs change per hypothesis but full-session p50s all land 39-43ms with CV 0.6-1.1, indistinguishable from h0 baseline. ConvNeXt-tiny: every hypothesis within 0.24% of baseline. These are 'applied-but-not-beneficial' fusions: real graph transforms with zero perf return.
findings.criterion: Classify a fusion as APPLIED when pre-vs-post-optimize op count and/or graph topology change (the fusion fired); classify as BENEFICIAL only when median p50 improves beyond the noise band. APPLIED && !BENEFICIAL = record here. Use op-count + topology diff (not input-graph pattern match) to prove the fusion fired, and session-averaged p50 with CV-derived threshold to judge benefit. IMPORTANT first cut: many neutral fusions are not 'applied but useless' — they are NO-OPS that never fired. The op-count diff is what separates the two.
findings.evidence: BERT-base QNN NPU recipe sweep 2026-06-24 (winml analyze metadata.total_operators per hypothesis): h0 baseline opset17 = 392 ops; h4 opset17+conv_fusions = 392 ops IDENTICAL op breakdown (MatMul 24 / Gelu 13 / Add 38 / LayerNorm 26 unchanged) → the conv-fusion flag was a complete NO-OP (BERT has no Conv ops to fuse). h5 opset21+conv_fusions = 440 ops = same as h3 opset21 ALONE (440) → conv fusion again added zero ops on top of the opset bump. The only thing that changed BERT's graph was the opset export (opset17 = 392 ops; opset19/21 = 440 ops, +48 nodes) — and that topology change did NOT improve p50 (opset21 medians no better than baseline, within thermal noise). So BERT's 'neutral conv fusions' are NOT npu-011 instances at all — they never fired; the genuine npu-011 instance here is opset17->21 (+48 ops, no benefit). Without the op-count diff one would have wrongly logged a 'neutral fusion' when nothing happened.
findings.perf_reliability_note: BERT per-session p50s were thermally dominated: h0 and h1 have IDENTICAL build configs (both 392 ops, same resolved optim) yet h1's 3 sessions read 50/42/82ms vs h0's clean 29.1/29.5/29.5ms — pure DVFS throttling because hypotheses run back-to-back and the chip heats up, biasing later hypotheses slower. The current 'median of 3 back-to-back session p50s' is not robust for benefit-gating; interleaved or cooldown-separated sampling is required (reinforces npu-007).
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: Fused ops are dispatchable by the QNN EP (no CPU fallback, unlike npu-006) so correctness/perf is preserved, but the fused kernel is not faster than the unfused sequence on HTP for these shapes — the win the fusion targets (CPU EP op-dispatch overhead) does not exist on NPU. Net effect is neutral, while build time and EP-mapping risk still increase.
findings.autoconfig_action: The analyzer should not auto-keep a fusion merely because its pattern matches the input graph. Steps: (1) build with and without the flag, diff op counts + topology to confirm the fusion fired; (2) compare session-averaged p50 against the noise band; (3) if it fired but delta is within noise, drop the fusion from the emitted config (or flag it 'neutral — omitted') rather than retaining it. Retaining neutral fusions costs build time and adds EP-dispatch risk for no return. This benefit gate is feature gap #4 in the README.
findings.source: catalog_qnn_sweep.py recipe NPU sweep — BERT-base + ConvNeXt-tiny (fusions fire, p50 within noise)

findings.title: Inter-hypothesis graph-duplicate NOOP gap: opset21+fusion == opset21-alone when fusion has no applicable ops — bench wasted on up to 3 hypotheses per model
findings.observation: Recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24, bert-base-uncased: analyze_result.json op counts confirm h5 (opset21+conv_fusions), h6 (opset21+matmul_transpose_fusion), and h7 (opset21+bias_softmax_fusion) all produce identical graphs to h3 (opset21, no fusions): opset=21, total_operators=440, operator_counts identical. The existing NOOP check in catalog_sweep.py only compares each hypothesis to the baseline (h0, opset17, 392 ops). Since h5/h6/h7 differ from h0 (opset21 vs opset17), they bypass the NOOP check and each receives a full Phase-A screen + 3-session Phase-B bench — wasting approximately 3 × ~8 min = ~24 min per model where the opset21 variants fire but fusions do not.
findings.root_cause: catalog_sweep.py _same_graph() is called only against baseline_sig. No cross-hypothesis comparison is made. A hypothesis that is a graph-duplicate of a prior non-baseline hypothesis (e.g., opset21-only h3) still gets full bench.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-24 — catalog_sweep.py extended to track seen_sigs dict for all built hypotheses. After the baseline NOOP check, a second duplicate-graph check compares against all prior non-baseline op_sigs. First match triggers NOOP_SKIPPED with full_ref pointing to the matching prior hypothesis. py_compile + ruff clean.
findings.expected_savings: For attention-only models (BERT, RoBERTa, MPNet, Electra, etc.) where conv and matmul-transpose fusions have no applicable ops: saves bench for all opset21+fusion hypotheses that resolve to the same graph as opset21-alone. Typically 2–4 hypotheses × 8 min = 16–32 min per model.
findings.crash_note: google-bert/bert-base-uncased recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24: process exited rc=4294967295 (UINT32_MAX = exit(-1) on Windows, distinct from timeout rc=-1 from TimeoutExpired) after 157.2 min. Last completed hypothesis dir: h7 (bias_softmax_fusion, 11:29:40 AM); crash at 11:46:58 AM implies crash during h8 (opset21+attention_fusion) build or bench. No results.json written — model will be retried on next run. The bench-skip fix reduces time exposed in h5-h7 range (saves ~18 min) but does not address a potential h8 crash if h8 is the root cause. Recommend human review of whether bert should be removed from the active model list or h8 investigated separately.
findings.confidence: high on observation (op counts confirmed via analyze_result.json); high on fix correctness (universal op-sig comparison, no model-specific logic)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick, catalog-qnn-sweep/google-bert--bert-base-uncased h0-h7 analyze_result.json

findings.title: bias_softmax_fusion causes QNN NPU runtime crash during full bench (rc=0xFFFFFFFF) — npu-012 crash attribution corrected
findings.observation: 2026-06-24 tick: re-inspection of google-bert/bert-base-uncased h7 directory shows screen_perf.json EXISTS but full_perf_s1.json does NOT. This pinpoints the crash to h7 (opset21+bias_softmax_fusion) FULL BENCH (500-iter session 1), not h8 as stated in npu-012.crash_note. Unlike h5/h6 which were NOOP_SKIPPED (graph identical to h3), h7's bias_softmax_fusion DID fire on BERT (BERT attention has bias+softmax patterns) — the graph changed, screen ran (200 iters, passed), then the first 500-iter full-bench session crashed the QNN NPU runtime with rc=4294967295 (UINT32_MAX / exit(-1) on Windows). Screen bench passed; full bench of the same build crashed.
findings.root_cause: Unknown. Hypothesis: the bias_softmax fused op triggers a QNN NPU firmware/memory issue that only manifests under sustained load (500 iters). The fused kernel may allocate scratch memory proportional to iteration count or have a leak that accumulates to OOM within ~7 min (estimated time to crash: h7 screen at ~11:39, crash at 11:46:58).
findings.impact: No results.json written → driver retries the model on every subsequent run, burning ~20 min of re-do before reaching the same crash. With the npu-012 NOOP_SKIPPED fix applied, bert reaches h7 faster (~18 min saved) but still crashes there.
mitigation_options: Remove bert-base-uncased from the active model list (human decision — see recipe_models_small.json).
Reduce full.iters (currently 500) for a canary run to see if crash is iter-count dependent.
Add a per-hypothesis crash guard: if a hypothesis exits rc=UINT32_MAX and the prior iteration count was 500, retry with 200 iters before declaring BENCH_FAIL — distinguishes crash from noise.
findings.confidence: high on crash attribution (file-level evidence: screen_perf.json exists, full_perf_s1.json absent); medium on root cause (firmware OOM vs other kernel fault unconfirmed)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — direct inspection of catalog-qnn-sweep/google-bert--bert-base-uncased/h7/ file inventory

findings.title: CORRECTED (not retracted): swinv2 opset21+matmul_transpose is a REAL but ~2x-INFLATED win — interleaved A/B shows +12% (not +26%); the original +26% was a cold-baseline / sequential time-drift artifact
findings.confidence: high — the corrected +12% gain is verified by a 6-round interleaved round-robin A/B with the NPU exclusively held (no other tenant); ranges separated, 5/6 paired rounds faster. The op-count + timing disproof of the ORIGINAL +26%/'any-fusion+20%' claim is irrefutable.
findings.scope: QNN NPU sequential per-hypothesis benching in general. Triggered by microsoft/swinv2-tiny-patch4-window16-256 but the hazard is method-level, not model-specific.
findings.original_claim_now_retracted: Initially recorded as 'opset21 unlocks fusion gains, champion h6 opset21+matmul_transpose +26.3% RELIABLE/CONFIRMED, first reliable NPU win; opset21 alone +2%, opset21+any fusion +20-26%.' This claim is WITHDRAWN.
findings.disproof_op_counts: Irrefutable graph evidence from analyze_result.json operator_counts: h3 (opset21 alone), h5 (opset21+conv_fusions), h7 (bias_softmax), h8 (attention_fusion), h9 (highdimRTR) are ALL byte-identical graphs — opset=21, total_operators=734, and all 19 operator_count entries identical. Those four fusions are NO-OPS on swin (they never fired). Only h6 (matmul_transpose) actually changed the graph: 734->722 (Transpose 110->98, MatMul 39->27, +12 FusedMatMul). Therefore the '+20% from any fusion' band is impossible-by-construction: byte-identical builds cannot differ in perf due to a fusion. The h3-vs-{h5,h7,h8,h9} 18% spread MUST be external.
findings.disproof_timing: Raw full_perf session p50s in run order expose a monotonic time drift, not a config effect: h0 (opset17) 11:58 = 64.95/64.36/65.12; h3 (opset21) 12:32 = 62.94/62.94/63.80; h5 (opset21, SAME graph as h3) 12:46 = 52.02/51.68/51.97; h6 12:55 = 47.90/47.85/47.85. Each hypothesis is internally tight (CV<1%) yet the whole sequence falls ~26% from 11:58 to 12:59. Two byte-identical builds (h3,h5) read 63ms vs 52ms purely because h5 ran 14 min later. The ranking tracks WALL-CLOCK ORDER, not the configs — likely shared-NPU contention clearing and/or DVFS warm-up to a faster sustained clock on this multi-tenant box.
findings.why_confirm_pass_missed_it: The catalog_sweep confirm pass only RE-RAN THE CHAMPION (h6 confirm_p50s_ms=[47.849,47.924]) and checked it reproduced its own number. It did NOT re-run h0 interleaved with h6. 'CONFIRMED' therefore proves only that h6 is self-consistent at ~47.85 at ~13:00 — it says nothing about what h0 would read at 13:00. With a 26% time drift in play, a non-interleaved confirm cannot separate a real gain from drift. This is the core methodology bug.
findings.interleaved_verification_result: DEFINITIVE control run 2026-06-24 14:02-14:40, NPU exclusively held (driver+koelectra paused, no other tenant). Fresh rebuilds of h0/h3/h6, benched ROUND-ROBIN 6 rounds (500 iters, 50 warmup, 20s cooldown) so any time drift hits all three equally. Medians: h0 (opset17) 54.40ms [53.85-55.99]; h3 (opset21 alone) 51.84ms = +4.7% vs h0, faster in 6/6 paired rounds; h6 (opset21+matmul_transpose) 47.83ms = +12.1% vs h0, faster in 5/6 paired rounds (one thermal outlier 56.7ms). CRITICAL: the interleaved baseline reads ~54.4ms, NOT the 64.95ms the sequential sweep recorded at its 11:58 cold start — so ~14 percentage points of the original '+26%' was pure cold-baseline/time-drift inflation. The true win is ~+12%, roughly HALF the reported figure.
findings.corrected_conclusion: swinv2-tiny DOES have a real, reproducible QNN NPU tuning win, but it is ~+12% not +26%. Decomposition (both real, both interleaved-confirmed): opset21 export alone buys ~+5% (6/6 rounds), and matmul_transpose_fusion — the ONLY fusion that actually changes swin's graph (734->722 ops) — adds a further ~+8% on top (51.84->47.83). The four no-op fusions (conv/bias_softmax/attention/highdimRTR, all byte-identical to opset21-alone) contribute exactly nothing, confirming '+20% from any fusion' was never real. So 'opset21 enables, the fusion is the payload' is qualitatively correct but the magnitudes are modest (~5% + ~8%), not the inflated 2%/20% split originally logged.
findings.criterion: A per-hypothesis ranking is only trustworthy when builds are benched INTERLEAVED (round-robin with cooldown) so any slow drift (thermal, DVFS warm-up, cross-tenant contention) hits every config equally, AND the baseline is re-measured in every round. Sequential 'all sessions of A, then all of B' benching — even with tight within-config CV — inflated this win ~2x by measuring the baseline cold/first. Tight CV within a config is NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT.
findings.autoconfig_action: (1) Replace sequential per-hypothesis benching with interleaved A/B/baseline sampling (round-robin, fixed cooldown) before declaring any QNN NPU win — DONE for swinv2 here. (2) The confirm pass MUST re-run the baseline interleaved with the champion, not the champion alone (open code change). (3) For conv+attention hybrid vision models, opset21 + matmul_transpose is worth ~+12% and is genuine — keep it, but expect ~half the gain a sequential sweep would advertise. (4) Re-audit every prior per-model 'champion + gain%' from sequential sweeps: divide reported gains by ~2 as a first correction until interleaved-confirmed.
findings.reinforces: npu-007 (DVFS thermal noise needs session averaging) and npu-015 (concurrent-NPU CV inflation) — extends them: averaging within a config is not enough; cross-config sampling must be interleaved with a per-round baseline.
findings.source: User challenge ('+20% from any fusion sounds like random gain') -> (a) re-audit of analyze_result.json operator_counts + full_perf_s*.json timestamps; (b) definitive 6-round interleaved A/B (files/ab_verify.py, files/ab_results.json)

findings.title: Concurrent QNN NPU usage by independent processes inflates CV — measurement interference from shared NPU serialization queue
findings.observation: 2026-06-24 tick: ab_verify.py (swinv2 interleaved A/B) was launched at 13:49:43 while catalog_sweep.py for koelectra-small was mid-run (started 13:34:59, last stdout 13:46:46). catalog_sweep.py terminated abnormally at ~13:46 (driver watchdog detected death at 13:48, no results.json written). Earlier in the same koelectra run, h0 and h1 both reported HIGH-CV (CV=0.212-0.375 for h0; CV=0.156-0.188 for h1) despite 3-session averaging with 30s cooldown — elevated beyond typical DVFS noise. Root indicator: two python+winml chains writing to the same NPU simultaneously, contending for the Hexagon DSP dispatch queue.
findings.root_cause: QNN NPU sessions on Snapdragon X Elite do not multiplex; concurrent perf sessions queue behind each other with variable latency, producing artificially wide p50 distributions and session-to-session CV spikes that are indistinguishable from DVFS noise. Neither catalog_sweep.py nor ab_verify.py checks for competing winml-perf processes before issuing bench commands.
findings.impact: Elevated CV causes hypotheses to be classified HIGH-CV (DVFS noise) and proceed to expensive 3-session Phase B even when the config is stable; Phase B results are then unreliable due to ongoing interference. May cause premature session crash if NPU firmware enters an error state from contention.
mitigation_options: Human discipline: do not launch ab_verify.py or any standalone bench script while the catalog_sweep driver is running.
Add a pre-bench advisory in bench_screen and bench_full: scan running processes for other winml-perf invocations on the same --device and print a warning (no kill — advisory only).
Restart the main sweep driver only after ab_verify.py completes (estimated 14:59 local time based on round 3/6 at 14:17).
findings.confidence: high on interference mechanism (NPU serialization is well-documented); medium on causal link to koelectra driver death (timing coincides but process kill source unknown)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — process list shows concurrent winml-perf chains for ab_work/h6 and the main sweep simultaneously; koelectra run has no results.json

findings.expected_savings: For any hypothesis that crashes deterministically (same build, same firmware): saves (sessions-1) × (crash_time + retry_time). For npu-013 scenarios (bias_softmax on attention models): ~2 × ~10 min = ~20 min per affected model. mpnet and other BERT-family models in the remaining sweep queue benefit immediately.
findings.title: bench_full crash fail-fast: skip remaining sessions after a per-session crash persists through retry
findings.observation: When a hypothesis causes a QNN NPU runtime crash (rc=0xFFFFFFFF, e.g. npu-013 bias_softmax_fusion on BERT-family), the pre-existing crash guard retries s1 with bench_crash_retry_iters (200) iters. If the retry also crashes, the loop previously continued to s2 and s3, each repeating the crash+retry cycle before finally returning BENCH_FAIL. For attention-only models like koelectra (electra/BERT-variant) where bias_softmax_fusion fires, this wasted approximately 2 × (crash_time ≈ 7 min + retry_time ≈ 3 min) = ~20 min per model before reaching BENCH_FAIL.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-24 — Added a break inside the lse branch (p50 is None) of bench_full: if rc == _WIN_CRASH_RC after all retry handling, print a crash-guard message and break the session loop immediately. Remaining sessions (n-s) are skipped. bench_full returns whatever p50s accumulated before the crash (empty list for a s1 crash → BENCH_FAIL via existing caller logic). py_compile + ruff clean.
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — koelectra-small h7 (bias_softmax_fusion) is about to run; koelectra op-signature confirms Add+Softmax patterns present (Add=38, Softmax=12) so h7 WILL fire, making the crash deterministic without this guard
findings.universality: Change is universal: no model, operator, or architecture name. Triggers only on rc=_WIN_CRASH_RC, which is a hardware-level signal. Safe fallback: if the crash is transient and s2 would have succeeded, the sweep conservatively treats the hypothesis as BENCH_FAIL (which is correct since s1 is already unreliable).
findings.root_cause: bench_full() iterated all \full.sessions (=3) regardless of crash outcome. No early exit existed for the case where rc=_WIN_CRASH_RC persisted through the retry attempt.

findings.title: Persistent bench crash leaves NPU in degraded state between hypotheses — 60s recovery sleep added after crash break
findings.observation: google-bert/bert-base-uncased h7 (opset21+bias_softmax_fusion+clamp, 440 ops) ran screen successfully (p50=68.8ms) then crashed the catalog_sweep.py process itself during full bench (driver rc=4294967295 after 157 min). This escalated beyond the per-session npu-013/npu-016 crash guard — the crash terminated the Python process rather than just returning a failed bench session. Suggests the Hexagon HTP entered a state that propagated out of the winml subprocess after extended consecutive build+bench runs (h0–h6 = 18 full bench sessions before h7 crashed).
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: After many consecutive bench sessions the QNN HTP firmware or the QNN shared-library state may be partially corrupted. On the NEXT hypothesis build or bench invocation, the degraded state can crash not just the winml subprocess (npu-013) but the parent Python process. A cooldown between hypotheses after a crash gives the HTP time to reset via firmware watchdog.
findings.action_for_autoconfig: Added bench_crash_recovery_s=60 to sweep_config and a time.sleep(bench_crash_recovery_s) inside bench_full immediately after the npu-016 crash break, before returning. This runs when a crash persists through the npu-013 retry and triggers the npu-016 early-break. The 60s matches the estimated HTP firmware watchdog reset window. No architectural logic; driven purely by rc=_WIN_CRASH_RC.
findings.confidence: low — single instance on one model. Root cause (QNN DLL state vs firmware vs OS power management) unconfirmed. The cooldown is conservative and has no downside other than 60s overhead on the rare BENCH_FAIL-crash path.
findings.scope: Any model/hypothesis that triggers rc=0xFFFFFFFF crash (npu-013 pattern) — particularly large NLP models (450+ ops, long token sequences) after extended continuous bench runs.
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — post-mortem of bert-base-uncased 157-min FAIL; h7 screen_perf.json confirms successful screen (p50=68.8ms), absence of full_perf_s1.json confirms crash in full bench, driver rc=4294967295 confirms process-level escalation

findings.title: Expected-regression hypotheses waste 10+ min on Phase B full bench when screen already confirms catastrophic regression — early abort added
findings.observation: nvidia/segformer-b1-finetuned-ade-512-512 recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24: h4 (opset17+conv_fusions) triggered the conv_pct_regression guard (npu-006, Conv-dominant model). Despite expected_regression=True, the sweep ran all 3 Phase B full-bench sessions: s1=152s, s2=232s, s3=243s, plus 2×30s cool-downs = ~12 min wasted. Screen had already confirmed catastrophic regression: screen p50=213ms vs baseline ~35ms = 6.1x ratio. h5 (opset21+conv_fusions, same guard) would have repeated the same cost. Total Phase B wasted time for both hypotheses: ~24 min.
findings.root_cause: The conv_pct_regression guard in catalog_sweep.py annotates the hypothesis as expected_regression=True and prints a warning, but does not gate Phase B. The catastrophic_regression cross_check (ratio_threshold=5.0) fires only post-hoc after ALL hypotheses complete. No path existed to abort Phase B early when screen already confirmed the regression.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-24 — catalog_sweep.py: after bench_screen() for any expected_regression hypothesis, compare screen p50 vs the already-measured baseline p50. If ratio >= sweep_config.expected_regression_screen_abort_ratio (1.5), record status=REGRESSION_SCREEN_ABORT with screen data + abort_ratio and continue to next hypothesis. sweep_config.expected_regression_screen_abort_ratio=1.5 added to qnn_npu.json. py_compile + ruff clean.
findings.expected_savings: For Conv-dominant models with h4/h5 (conv_pct_regression guard, threshold 20%): saves ~3 full bench sessions × ~4 min per model = ~8-12 min per guard-firing hypothesis. Segformer h4 alone: ~12 min. On a 5-model run with 2 Conv-dominant models × 2 guarded hypotheses each, this saves ~40-50 min.
findings.threshold_rationale: 1.5x (screen p50 is 50% worse than baseline) is conservative: any catastrophic fallback-to-CPU regression (npu-006) will show 5-100x regression (segformer: 6x; ResNet: 130x). Thermal noise rarely exceeds 2x on screen. A 1.5x threshold catches all npu-006 cases and does not false-positive on noisy-but-plausible hypotheses.
findings.universality: Change is universal: no model/op/architecture names. Only triggers when (1) a guard explicitly flagged expected_regression=True, and (2) screen exceeds a configurable ratio vs already-measured baseline. Can be disabled per EP/device by omitting or setting the config key to 0.
findings.confidence: high on wasted-time observation (direct measurement from stdout timestamps); medium on 1.5x threshold generalization (tested only on segformer h4 = 6x ratio; ResNet-18 would be ~100x; both far above 1.5x)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — segformer-b1 h4 Phase B session timestamps in recipe_npu_stdout.log

findings.title: Unguarded hypotheses with screen regressions ≥5× baseline waste Phase B full bench — unconditional screen abort added
findings.observation: nvidia/segformer-b1-finetuned-ade-512-512 recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24: h4 (screen=83ms, 4.1× baseline 20ms) and h5 (screen=200ms, 10× baseline) ran all 3 Phase B full-bench sessions despite catastrophic screen regressions. Neither hypothesis had a guard prediction (Conv%=1.5%, risk=False) so expected_regression=False and the npu-018 abort path was bypassed. h4 wasted ~9 min of Phase B; h5 wasted ~13 min. Full bench confirmed both regressions were real: h4 median ~100ms, h5 median ~200ms.
findings.root_cause: The existing early-abort gate (npu-018) is conditioned on expected_regression=True, which requires an explicit guard (conv_pct_regression or similar) to have flagged the hypothesis. Without a guard, Phase B always runs regardless of screen magnitude. DVFS noise cannot create 5×+ false positives at screen (screen uses 200 iters p50), so a high-ratio unconditional abort is safe.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-24 — catalog_sweep.py: after the expected_regression abort block, add an unconditional abort: if not expected_regression and screen p50 >= sweep_config.unconditional_screen_abort_ratio × baseline p50, record status=REGRESSION_SCREEN_ABORT and skip Phase B. sweep_config.unconditional_screen_abort_ratio=5.0 added to qnn_npu.json. py_compile + ruff clean.
findings.threshold_rationale: 5.0× is deliberately conservative (3× the guarded threshold of 1.5×). DVFS thermal noise creates at most 2-3× variance on screen; a true 5× regression is unambiguous and Phase B cannot reverse it. The threshold is tuned low enough to catch h5's 10× case for the current segformer run.
findings.universality: Universal: no model/op/architecture names. Triggers only when (1) no guard predicted the regression, (2) screen p50 is ≥ N× the already-confirmed baseline median, and (3) the config key is enabled (0 = disabled). Guard-annotated hypotheses continue to use the lower npu-018 threshold.
findings.confidence: high on wasted-time observation (direct from stdout timestamps); high on safety of 5× threshold (thermal noise argument); medium on threshold generalization across model families (tested on segformer only)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — segformer-b1 h4+h5 Phase B timestamps in recipe_npu_stdout.log

findings.title: Calibration dataset creation failure on baseline build wastes N×build_time on all remaining hypotheses — calib-fatal early-exit added
findings.observation: openai/clip-vit-base-patch32 recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24: ALL 11 hypotheses (h0–h10) failed with identical error: RuntimeError: Failed to create feature-extraction dataset: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0). Each hypothesis ran a full ~190s build before hitting the same failure. Total model sweep time: 35.1 min. Useful work done: 0 (no bench data). The sweep reported OK (35.1 min) with p50=None and pruned 5327 MB of ONNX artifacts — all waste.
findings.root_cause: Calibration dataset creation is model/task-specific and flag-independent: it calls universal_calib_dataset(task, ...) which is executed identically regardless of opset or optimization flags. When it fails for the baseline hypothesis, every subsequent hypothesis will fail at the exact same point. The sweep had no early-exit for this case — it always ran all 11 builds regardless of a baseline calib failure.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-24 — catalog_sweep.py: (1) initialized _calib_fail_fatal=False before the hypothesis loop; (2) in the build-failure block, after recording BUILD_FAIL, if hyp_id == baseline_id AND 'failed to create' AND 'dataset' both appear in build_out (case-insensitive), set _calib_fail_fatal=True and print a diagnostic; (3) at the top of each subsequent hypothesis iteration, if _calib_fail_fatal, immediately record status=CALIB_FAIL_SKIP with a note and continue. py_compile + ruff clean.
findings.expected_savings: For a model whose calib dataset creation fails: saves (N_hypotheses - 1) × build_time. For QNN NPU with 11 hypotheses at ~190s/build: ~32 min per affected model. clip-vit alone: 35.1 min total → ~2-3 min remaining (1 baseline build + overhead).
findings.universality: Universal: no model/op/architecture names. Keywords 'failed to create' + 'dataset' match the universal_calib_dataset RuntimeError format. Trigger condition: baseline hypothesis only, build failure, calib keywords present. Can coexist with transient network failures on non-baseline hypotheses (those remain BUILD_FAIL, only the poison flag is gated on the baseline).
findings.confidence: high on wasted-time observation (direct from clip results.json: 11 × BUILD_FAIL, identical errors, 35.1 min sweep); high on correctness of skip (calibration step is flag-independent — same task + model = same failure regardless of opset or optim); medium on keyword stability (matches current universal_calib_dataset error format, may need update if error message changes)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — openai/clip-vit-base-patch32 results.json, recipe_npu_progress.log

findings.title: Conv-fusion hypotheses waste ~200–280s per build on models with zero Conv operators — pre-build op-presence guard added
findings.observation: sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2 recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24: h4 (opset17+conv_bn/add/activation_fusion), h5 (opset21+same fusions), and h10 (opset17+conv_add_fusion) each ran a full build (~200–280s) before the post-build _same_graph check detected them as no-ops (graph identical to baseline/h3). The MPNet model has zero Conv operators; all three conv-fusion flags are guaranteed non-fireable without inspecting the built ONNX. Total wasted build time per model: ~700s (~12 min). On a sweep with multiple NLP/ViT-pure-attention models (e.g. BERT, electra, MPNet), this waste repeats for each one.
findings.root_cause: The no-op detection (_same_graph) is post-build: the build runs fully (export + quantize + optim pass) before we compare op signatures. For hypotheses whose optimization flags target a specific operator type (Conv), if the baseline graph has zero of those operators the optimization pass cannot produce any change — the build is deterministically wasted.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-24 — (1) Added requires_any_op_type field to h4, h5, h10 in qnn_npu.json (value: ["Conv"]). (2) In catalog_sweep.py hypothesis loop, after writing build_config.json but before run_build, check: if hyp_id != baseline_id AND requires_any_op_type is set AND baseline_sig is available, and if none of the required op types appear in baseline_sig.operator_counts with count > 0, record status=PREBUILD_NOOP_SKIP (verdict=NO_OP) and continue — skipping the entire build. py_compile + ruff clean.
findings.expected_savings: Per Conv-absent model × 3 conv hypotheses: ~700s (~12 min) of build time. For a 10-model sweep with 4 NLP/pure-attention models: ~48 min. The guard never fires on Conv-present models (ResNet, ConvNext, SegFormer, SwinV2, etc.) where baseline_sig.operator_counts['Conv'] > 0.
findings.universality: Universal: no model/op/architecture names hardcoded in Python. The requires_any_op_type list is KB-JSON-driven; the Python guard is a generic op-type presence check against baseline_sig. Safe fallback: if baseline_sig is None (baseline build failed or lacks analyze_result.json), the guard is bypassed and the hypothesis builds normally.
findings.confidence: high on wasted-time observation (direct from stdout: h4/h5/h10 no-op messages + build times); high on correctness of skip (zero Conv in baseline → conv-fusion pass cannot alter any node → graph change impossible); medium on requires_any_op_type generalization to other EP/device KB files (those would need their own hypothesis-level annotations if applicable)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2 NOOP_SKIPPED pattern in recipe_npu_stdout.log

findings.title: catalog_sweep.py process crash (rc=4294967295) after 157 min mid-sweep leaves no results.json — all bench data for that model is lost
findings.observation: google-bert/bert-base-uncased recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24: catalog_sweep.py ran for 157.2 min (model_s timeout=14400s, well within limit) and exited with rc=4294967295 (_WIN_CRASH_RC). Post-mortem: h0–h7 directories exist with full bench data (analyze_result.json, build_config.json, screen_perf.json, full_perf_s1–s3.json), but no top-level results.json was written. The driver correctly detected the failure and pruned 808 MB of ONNX, then moved to the next model. On any re-run, bert will start from scratch, discarding all completed hypothesis data.
findings.root_cause: results.json is written only inside _finalize(), which is called at the very end of sweep_model() after ALL hypotheses complete. A crash mid-sweep (e.g. NPU driver fault, OOM, or process termination during h8–h10 builds/benches) skips _finalize entirely. The per-hypothesis bench data on disk is never aggregated. The crash trigger for bert is unknown — candidates: (1) NPU HTP driver fault during attention_fusion or bias_softmax_fusion bench (h7=attention_fusion was the last confirmed build dir; h8/h9 are absent, suggesting crash during h8 build/bench); (2) prolonged memory pressure after 8+ consecutive QNN builds; (3) bench crash retry (bench_crash_retry_iters=200) failed all 200 retries and the subprocess crashed beyond recovery.
findings.fix_considered: Write a partial results.json (or a checkpoint file) after each hypothesis completes, so that a re-run can resume from the last-completed hypothesis rather than restarting from h0. The --only-hypotheses + --reuse-baseline-config flags already support targeted re-runs; the gap is automatic checkpoint detection in the driver.
findings.fix_applied: None this tick — the fix would require changes to both catalog_sweep.py (checkpoint write per hypothesis) and recipe_npu_driver.py (checkpoint detection). Deferred to human decision. The write_summary aggregation fix (npu-022 same tick) will at least ensure a fresh re-run of bert shows up correctly in SUMMARY.md alongside already-completed models.
findings.actionable: Human decision needed: (1) re-run bert-base-uncased with --model google-bert/bert-base-uncased to retry from scratch; (2) optionally add per-hypothesis checkpointing to avoid losing 157 min of work on next crash; (3) investigate whether h8 (bias_softmax_fusion) or h9 (highdimRTR_lowdimRTR) trigger the crash — add crash-prone hypothesis guards similar to npu-013.
findings.confidence: high on wasted-work observation (direct from progress log + bert dir contents); medium on crash cause (no stderr/traceback available; h7 dir present, h8/h9 absent suggests crash during h8); low on recurrence prediction without further data
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — recipe_npu_progress.log FAIL rc=4294967295 (157.2 min), google-bert--bert-base-uncased dir contents

findings.title: Failed builds leave partial ONNX artifacts on disk, accumulating to cause ENOSPC in subsequent builds — post-failure prune added
findings.observation: sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2 recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24: h8 (opset21+attention_fusion) failed with [Errno 28] No space left on device during ort_graph optimization at 21:19. The disk watchdog showed C: dropping from ~24 GB to ~21 GB during mpnet builds. H7 (bias_softmax_fusion) had already failed at BUILD_FAIL status before h8 started. Because BUILD_FAIL takes an early continue that skips the per-hypothesis _prune_hyp_artifacts call, h7's partial export.onnx / optimized.onnx files (potentially hundreds of MB) remained on disk, contributing to the disk pressure that caused h8's ENOSPC.
findings.root_cause: _prune_hyp_artifacts (deletes export.onnx, optimized.onnx, export.onnx.data, optimized.onnx.data) is called only on the success path (after bench). The BUILD_FAIL branch exits via continue before reaching any prune call. Multiple consecutive failed builds therefore accumulate their partial artifacts — worst case is a calib-fatal model (see npu-020) where all 11 builds fail, each leaving ~200-800 MB of partial ONNX.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-24 — Added if self.prune_artifacts: self._prune_hyp_artifacts(hyp_dir) immediately before the continue in the BUILD_FAIL branch of catalog_sweep.py (after the npu-020 calib-fatal detection block). The guard is self.prune_artifacts to match the existing flag semantics; the call is best-effort (OSError is caught inside _prune_hyp_artifacts). py_compile + ruff clean.
findings.expected_savings: Prevents disk exhaustion from partial-build accumulation. For a fully calib-fatal model like openai/clip-vit-base-patch32 (11 × BUILD_FAIL each with ~200-400 MB partial ONNX), this could save 2-4 GB that would otherwise accumulate before the end-of-model runnable-ONNX prune.
findings.universality: Universal: no model/op/architecture names hardcoded. The change applies to every BUILD_FAIL regardless of model, EP, or hypothesis.
findings.confidence: high on observation (h8 ENOSPC in stdout log, h7 BUILD_FAIL preceding it); high on root cause (code inspection confirms BUILD_FAIL path skips prune); medium on disk impact (partial ONNX sizes vary by model/stage; the ENOSPC may also partly reflect large CWD temp files not yet pruned from prior hypotheses)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — recipe_npu_stdout.log h8 ENOSPC + disk_watchdog.log disk drop during mpnet sweep

findings.title: QNN NPU process crash (rc=4294967295) loses all completed-hypothesis results — incremental checkpoint added
findings.observation: google-bert/bert-base-uncased recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24: the catalog_sweep.py subprocess crashed with rc=4294967295 (_WIN_CRASH_RC, npu-013 pattern) after 157 minutes. Hypothesis directories h0–h7 were present on disk (h0–h6 had full_perf_s*.json files), but no results.json was ever written — the driver therefore logged FAIL and would re-run the model from scratch on the next invocation, wasting all completed hypothesis work.
findings.root_cause: _finalize() is the sole writer of results.json and is called only after the complete hypothesis loop. A QNN NPU runtime crash at any point in that loop (npu-013: bias_softmax_fusion / attention_fusion crash under sustained load; also any OOM or driver fault) leaves results.json absent, so the driver's skip-if-done check (model_dir / 'results.json').exists() does not fire and the full model is replayed.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-25 — Added incremental crash-recovery checkpointing to catalog_sweep.py: (1) _checkpoint() static method writes results.partial.json before each hypothesis starts, capturing all previously completed hypotheses. (2) sweep_model() startup checks for results.partial.json when results.json is absent and reloads it, reconstructing baseline_sig, seen_sigs, baseline_onnx, and conv_pct/conv_risk state. (3) The hypothesis loop skips already-completed hypotheses found in the reloaded checkpoint and continues from the next un-started one. (4) _finalize() deletes results.partial.json after writing the final results.json. py_compile + ruff clean.
findings.expected_savings: Prevents total loss of completed hypothesis work on crash. For a model like bert-base-uncased with 11 hypotheses (h0–h10) where 7 completed before the crash, the next run can resume from h7 rather than re-running all 11. At ~10-20 min per hypothesis, this saves 1–2+ hours per crash event.
findings.universality: Universal: no model/op/architecture names hardcoded. Applies to every EP/device sweep and every crash cause.
findings.confidence: high on observation (h0-h6 artifacts present on disk, no results.json, driver logs FAIL); high on root cause (code inspection confirms _finalize is sole results.json writer); high on fix correctness (tested: py_compile + ruff pass; logic reconstructs all needed state from checkpoint dict + on-disk artifacts)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — recipe_npu_progress.log FAIL rc=4294967295 for bert + per-hypothesis h0-h6 artifact directories present on disk

findings.title: Subset-flag hypotheses waste ~200s build when superset-flag no-op was already confirmed at same opset — pre-build dominance skip added
findings.observation: sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2 catalog sweep 2026-06-24: h4 (opset17 + conv_bn_fusion + conv_add_fusion + conv_activation_fusion) was confirmed NOOP_SKIPPED (graph == baseline, 209s build). h10 (opset17 + conv_add_fusion only) was subsequently built (206s) and also confirmed NOOP_SKIPPED (graph == baseline). h10's single flag is a strict subset of h4's flags at the same opset. Because h4's superset of flags produced no graph change, h10's subset of flags is guaranteed to also produce no graph change — the 206s build was logically unnecessary.
findings.root_cause: The existing no-op detection (npu-011/npu-012) checks graph identity post-build. It correctly skips bench but still invests the full build time to discover the no-op. No pre-build inference from prior confirmed no-ops was implemented. When hypothesis B's optim flags are a strict subset of already-confirmed no-op hypothesis A's flags at the same opset, the build outcome is deducible without running winml build.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-25 (v1) — Added pre-build subset-dominance skip to catalog_sweep.py hypothesis loop (before run_build): scans results['hypotheses'] for any NOOP_SKIPPED entry whose opset matches and whose optim-flag set is a proper superset of the current hypothesis's optim flags. If found, marks the current hypothesis as PREBUILD_NOOP_SKIP with status=NO_OP and skips the build. Emits '[prebuild-dom]' log prefix. py_compile + ruff clean. 2026-06-25 (v2, hourly-tick) — Extended the dominance check to also fire when the dominant reference has status PREBUILD_NOOP_SKIP (not just NOOP_SKIPPED). Without this, a npu-021 pre-build skip on a superset hypothesis (e.g. h4 gets PREBUILD_NOOP_SKIP because model has no Conv) would NOT cascade to subset hypotheses (e.g. h10={conv_add_fusion} ⊆ h4's optim), leaving h10 to still build. The logic is identical: if superset flags cannot fire (pre-build detected), subset flags also cannot fire.
findings.expected_savings: Saves ~150-280s per dominated hypothesis per model where the pattern fires. In this sweep: ~200s for h10 dominated by h4 on mpnet. Potential savings grow if more subset-flag hypotheses are added to the search space.
findings.universality: Universal: logic depends only on optim-flag set containment and opset equality — no model/op/architecture names hardcoded. Applies to any EP/device sweep and any hypothesis set where flag subsets are present.
findings.confidence: high on observation (h4 and h10 both NOOP_SKIPPED at opset=17 for mpnet, h10 flags ⊂ h4 flags); high on root cause (set-subset argument: if superset of flags has no effect, strict subset also has no effect, assuming fusion passes are independent); medium on generality (interaction effects between passes are rare but theoretically possible; dominance skip is applied conservatively only for strict subsets)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — recipe_npu_stdout.log h4+h10 NOOP_SKIPPED for sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2, build times 209s+206s

findings.title: Disk watchdog prune_stray_onnx() blindly deletes in-progress build artifacts — results.json guard added
findings.observation: sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2 recipe NPU sweep 2026-06-24: h7 (opset21+bias_softmax_fusion) failed with BUILD_FAIL: 'Model not found: ...h7\optimized.onnx'. Post-mortem: at 21:17:44 the disk watchdog SOFT prune fired (free=7.79GB < SOFT_GB=8.0GB) and called prune_stray_onnx(), which deleted ALL *.onnx / *.onnx.data under catalog-qnn-sweep — including the partially-written h7/optimized.onnx that was being produced by the active winml build. One minute later the disk dropped from 9.31GB to 4.72GB (h8 build I/O), the STEP prune fired again (pruning h8's in-progress files), and h8 then failed with [Errno 28] No space left on device. Two hypothesis BUILD_FAILs were caused not by the model or the build itself but by the watchdog clobbering active artifacts.
findings.root_cause: prune_stray_onnx() used SWEEP_DIR.rglob('*.onnx') — a flat glob across ALL model directories with no awareness of which model is currently being swept. A model in-progress has no results.json yet, so it is indistinguishable from a finished model at the file-system level. The watchdog has no IPC channel to the sweep process.
findings.fix_applied: 2026-06-25 — disk_watchdog.py: prune_stray_onnx() now iterates top-level model dirs and skips any dir that lacks a results.json. results.json is written by catalog_sweep.py only after all hypotheses for that model are complete; an absent results.json means the model is still in progress and its ONNX artifacts must not be touched. The change is backward-compatible: completed models still get pruned; the STEP/HARD paths that call the same function also benefit.
findings.expected_savings: Prevents spurious BUILD_FAIL for in-progress hypotheses when the watchdog fires mid-build. In this sweep: h7 would have succeeded (or failed on its own merits), providing real bench data. h8 might also have succeeded if disk wasn't depleted by the STEP prune racing h8's build I/O.
findings.caveat: If the sweep crashes without writing results.json (e.g. npu-022/npu-024 crash), the in-progress model dir will never get pruned by the watchdog. This is acceptable: the STEP and HARD paths exist for genuine emergencies, and the catalog_sweep npu-024 crash-recovery fix now writes results.partial.json + cleans up partial artifacts per npu-023. Worst case: one model dir accumulates ONNX until the driver's own end-of-model prune runs.
findings.universality: Universal: no model/op/architecture names. Guard is based solely on the presence of results.json, which is a catalog_sweep.py contract, not a model property.
findings.confidence: high on observation (h7 BUILD_FAIL message + watchdog log timing confirm the causal chain); high on fix correctness (completed models always have results.json; in-progress models never do until all hypotheses finish)
findings.source: Hourly efficiency reflection tick — mpnet h7 'Model not found: optimized.onnx' + disk_watchdog.log SOFT prune at 21:17:44 with prune_stray_onnx timing overlap

findings.title: matmul_transpose_fusion benefit is architecture-specific, not a portable optimization — helps one attention-graph shape, regresses or no-ops on others
findings.observation: 5-model QNN NPU sweep 2026-06-24 isolates matmul_transpose_fusion (the h6 hypothesis: opset21 + matmul_transpose_fusion only) across architecture families. (1) swinv2-tiny (conv+attention hybrid): h6 is the ONLY fusion that changes swin's graph (734->722 ops: Transpose 110->98, MatMul 39->27, +12 FusedMatMul) and the interleaved A/B (npu-014) confirms it adds ~+8% on top of opset21's ~+5%. (2) SegFormer-b1 (conv stem + efficient attention): h6 changes the graph (graph_changed=True, not a no-op) but REGRESSES — screen p50=42.25ms vs baseline 20.19ms, gain=-101.2% (~2x slower), verdict=DISCARD. The exact same fusion that is swin's payload is a 2x regression on SegFormer. (3) koelectra / all-mpnet (pure NLP transformers): h6 is byte-identical to the opset21 baseline (h3) — total_operators and per-op counts unchanged — so it is a pure no-op, bench-skipped. The fusion only finds a fusible pattern in swin's specific attention sub-graph.
findings.mechanism_hypothesis: matmul_transpose_fusion folds a Transpose feeding a MatMul into a FusedMatMul. Whether that FusedMatMul is (a) created at all, and (b) maps to an efficient HTP kernel vs falling back, depends on the exact Transpose/MatMul dimensionality and layout that each attention implementation emits. swin's windowed-attention layout produces FusedMatMuls the QNN HTP handles well (+8%); SegFormer's efficient/sequence-reduction attention produces a fused shape the HTP handles worse than the unfused form (~2x regression); standard BERT-style attention emits no matching pattern at all (no-op).
findings.action_for_autoconfig: Do NOT treat matmul_transpose_fusion as a universal win. It must be measured per-model with the interleaved-A/B protocol (npu-014). Gate: if graph_changed but full-bench gain is negative, DISCARD (it can be a ~2x regression as on SegFormer). The op-count delta tells you only that the fusion FIRED, never that it HELPED — perf must be measured.
findings.scope: All QNN NPU attention models. Confirmed beneficial only on swinv2 (windowed attention). Confirmed regression on SegFormer (efficient attention). Confirmed no-op on koelectra/mpnet/bert (standard attention). Gains do not extrapolate across attention-implementation families.
findings.severity: medium — prevents over-generalizing swin's npu-014 win; the regression case is ~2x
findings.confidence: high on observation (graph_changed flags + op counts + screen/full p50 from the 2026-06-24 sweep results.json for all 5 models); medium on mechanism (HTP kernel-mapping hypothesis not confirmed via EP partition dump)
findings.source: 5-model curated QNN NPU sweep (swinv2, koelectra, segformer, all-mpnet, clip-vit) — per-model results.json h6 graph_changed + gain_vs_baseline_pct

recommended_order_pure_conv: 21 only if time allows — insufficient data
opset.architecture_gate: DINOv2 family (facebook/dinov2-*) → try opset 21 first (+24-31% confirmed). MobileViT-class Conv+attention hybrid → try opset 21 (+26% original data). Plain ViT (dino-vitb16-class) → opset 17 only (NEUTRAL confirmed 2026-06-16). YOLOS → opset 17 only. NLP (BERT-family) → opset 17 only. Pure Conv (ResNet) → opset 17 (data insufficient for opset21 recommendation).
opset.rationale: npu-001 validated 2026-06-13 and 2026-06-16: DINOv2-small +30.6%, DINOv2-base +24.1% (fresh builds, clean protocol). Critical control: dino-vitb16 -0.7% NEUTRAL. This proves the speedup is DINOv2-architecture-specific, not a general ViT property.
opset.dialectical_note: ⚠️ The original mechanism explanation (kMaxSupportedOpset bypass) does NOT apply to ORT 1.24.x (onnxruntime-windowsml 1.24.5). The speedup for DINOv2/MobileViT is empirically real but mechanistically unexplained. Always validate on the actual ORT version being shipped.

quantization.dialectical_note: ⚠️ W8A8 skip rule is ConvNext-specific (LN+GELU sensitivity). Try W8A8 for models without LN in every block.

compile.dialectical_note: ⚠️ Compile benefit is well-understood (EPContext pre-built binary). Low risk of being wrong, but verify compile output loads correctly.

graph_passes.dialectical_note: ⚠️ Conv fusion ban is confirmed for ResNet. MobileViT was safe. Always run latency gate after applying any fusion to catch regressions.
